Scenario.title: SolarWinds Compromise Energy Sector Attack Scenario
Scenario.overview: Energy Intermediate The attackers compromise energy management systems, gaining remote access and manipulating energy grid operations to cause widespread power outages and financial losses.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Custom APIs, Energy Grid Operations, Shared DB or Directory, Internet-facing assets, Injection Flaws, SSL Certificates and Attribution
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Account Takeover, API Abuse, Ransomware, Code Injection, Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM), Vulnerability Exploit

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-657: Malicious Automated Software Update via Spoofing
stage.description: The attackers create a fake software update for energy management systems, exploiting a previously unknown vulnerability in the software.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: The attackers use the compromised software update to gain access to energy management systems' APIs, allowing them to manipulate energy grid operations remotely.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attackers inject malicious code into the energy management systems' software, ensuring long-term persistence and control over the systems.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-429: Target Influence via Eye Cues
stage.description: The attackers move laterally within the energy company's network, using compromised credentials to access sensitive areas and escalate privileges.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: To evade detection, the attackers intercept and decrypt SSL/TLS communications between energy management systems and the energy company's servers, making it appear as if the communication is legitimate.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The attackers use their access to disrupt energy grid operations, causing widespread power outages and financial losses for the energy company.

Scenario.summary: The SolarWinds Compromise energy sector attack scenario involves expert attackers compromising energy management systems, gaining remote access, and manipulating energy grid operations to cause widespread power outages and financial losses.
